title: Ingots: Discontinuities and Ultrasonic Response
narrationScript: Almost every plate, bar, forging, and pipe you will ever put a probe on began as a large casting. Either the steel was teemed into a mold and solidified as an ingot, or it was continuously cast into a strand and cut into slabs and blooms. Either way, the discontinuities that matter in the finished product are usually born in those first minutes of solidification. If you understand how an ingot freezes, you can predict what is inside it, where it sits, and which way it is oriented. That prediction is the heart of Level Two evaluation work. Start with the freeze itself. Molten steel poured into a cast iron mold begins to solidify at the mold wall, where heat leaves fastest. A thin skin of fine, randomly oriented grains forms first, called the chill zone. From that skin, long columnar grains grow inward, perpendicular to the mold wall, following the direction of heat flow. The last metal to freeze is the central zone near the top, where coarse, equiaxed grains form. Three things concentrate in that last liquid. Shrinkage, because steel contracts several percent as it solidifies. Gas, because dissolved oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen come out of solution as the metal freezes. And solute elements like carbon, sulfur, and phosphorus, which the growing crystals reject into the remaining liquid. Shrinkage in the last liquid produces pipe, a cavity along the top centerline of the ingot. Primary pipe is open to the top surface and usually oxidized. Secondary pipe forms deeper, as a closed internal cavity. Mills fight pipe with an insulated hot top that keeps a reservoir of liquid metal feeding the shrinkage, and they crop the top of the ingot before rolling. But if the crop is short, pipe travels into the wrought product, and because its walls are oxidized it will not weld shut during rolling. Gas produces porosity and blowholes, and how much depends on deoxidation practice. Fully killed steel has been deoxidized with silicon and aluminum, so it freezes quietly with little gas, but it shows a larger shrinkage pipe. Rimmed steel is barely deoxidized. Carbon and oxygen react during the freeze and evolve carbon monoxide, producing a rim of clean metal around a core full of blowholes and heavy segregation. Semi killed and capped grades sit in between. Nonmetallic inclusions come in two families. Indigenous inclusions form inside the steel itself, as deoxidation products such as aluminum oxides, silicates, and sulfides. Exogenous inclusions come from outside, as entrained slag, eroded refractory, or mold splash. Lighter inclusions float toward the top of the ingot, and many are trapped along the boundaries between columnar grains. Segregation, the uneven distribution of alloying and impurity elements, concentrates near the top center as well. And thermal stress during cooling can tear the metal internally along the weak columnar grain boundaries, producing internal cracks called hot tears. Now put sound into this material. As cast structure is coarse grained, and coarse grains scatter ultrasound. The baseline fills with grass, the back reflection weakens, and a five megahertz probe may be useless. The standard answer is to drop the frequency, often to one megahertz or two and a quarter megahertz, trading some sensitivity for a workable signal to noise ratio. Each discontinuity answers differently. Pipe lies along the ingot axis, so a straight beam from the side strikes it broadside and returns a strong, distinct echo with a clear loss of back reflection behind it. Porosity is a cloud of small reflectors, so it returns a cluster of low amplitude echoes and pulls the back reflection down without producing one clean signal. Inclusions give sharp, isolated little echoes, although a large piece of trapped slag can reflect like a real planar flaw. Segregation is the quiet one. It is solid metal, not a void, so it returns little or no direct echo. Sometimes the only clue is a local change in attenuation or velocity. Evaluation follows the purchase specification or the governing standard. Most ingot and bloom stage examinations use straight beam, contact or immersion, with sensitivity tied to a flat bottom hole reference or to a percentage of back reflection, and acceptance based on the amplitude of the indication and on how much back reflection is lost. Record what the specification tells you to record, location, amplitude, and extent. By the end of this lesson you should be able to look at an ingot sketch and call your shots, what lives where, which way it is oriented, and what it will look like on the screen.
imagePrompt: A steelworker in silver heat-resistant gear watches a glowing orange ingot being stripped from its cast-iron mold in a dark melt shop, overhead crane tongs gripping the hot block.

sections.heading: From Melt to Ingot: Why Casting Matters to UT
sections.body:
Nearly every wrought product you will test — plate, bar, forging, pipe — began as a large casting. In the traditional route, molten steel is **teemed** from a ladle into cast-iron ingot molds; in the modern route it is **continuously cast** into a strand that is cut into slabs and blooms. Either way, the part's discontinuity population is largely decided during those first minutes of solidification.

An ingot freezes from the outside in. Metal touching the cold mold wall solidifies almost instantly into a thin skin of fine, randomly oriented grains — the **chill zone**. From that skin, long **columnar grains** grow inward, perpendicular to the mold wall, following the heat flow. The last region to freeze is the coarse **equiaxed zone** at the top center.

Three things concentrate in that last-to-freeze liquid:

- **Shrinkage** — steel contracts several percent on solidification, so the final liquid cannot feed itself.
- **Gas** — dissolved oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen come out of solution as the metal freezes.
- **Solute** — carbon, sulfur, and phosphorus are rejected by the growing crystals and pile up in the remaining melt.

Hold that picture: the top center is the neighborhood where trouble concentrates, and the vertical centerline is its main street.

sections.caption: Vertical section of a big-end-up ingot showing the chill, columnar, and equiaxed zones with the typical homes of pipe, porosity, inclusions, segregation, and hot tears.

sections.heading: Shrinkage Pipe and Gas Porosity
sections.body:
**Pipe** is the cavity left when the last top-center liquid solidifies with nothing left to feed it. **Primary pipe** opens to the top surface and oxidizes; **secondary pipe** forms lower down as a closed internal cavity. Mills fight it with an insulated **hot top** that keeps a reservoir of liquid feeding the shrinkage, then **crop** the ingot top before rolling. A short crop lets pipe travel into the wrought product — and because its walls are oxidized, it will not weld shut during hot working. Rolled flat, it becomes the classic centerline lamination.

How much gas trouble an ingot carries depends on **deoxidation practice**:

- **Killed steel** — fully deoxidized with silicon and aluminum; freezes quietly with few blowholes but a deeper pipe (hence the hot top).
- **Semi-killed** — partially deoxidized; modest pipe, some scattered blowholes.
- **Rimmed steel** — little deoxidation; carbon and oxygen react during freezing and evolve carbon monoxide. The rimming action builds a clean low-carbon rim around a core rich in **blowholes** and segregation.
- **Capped** — rimming started, then arrested with a cap; behavior between rimmed and semi-killed.

**Blowholes and porosity** are rounded gas cavities — singles, clusters, or bands. Subsurface blowholes with clean walls may forge-weld shut during rolling; oxidized ones survive as flattened discontinuities. Hydrogen picked up in the melt shop can also stay dissolved and cause **flakes** later in heavy forged or rolled sections — internal fissures we will meet again in bar stock.

sections.heading: Inclusions, Segregation, and Internal Cracks
sections.body:
**Nonmetallic inclusions** come in two families. **Indigenous** inclusions form in the melt itself — deoxidation products such as alumina and silicates, plus manganese sulfides. They are small, numerous, and unavoidable; melt-shop cleanliness controls their size and count. **Exogenous** inclusions arrive from outside: entrained ladle slag, eroded refractory brick, loose mold material or splash. They are larger, rarer, and worse. Lighter inclusions float toward the ingot top, and many are trapped along the boundaries between columnar grains, giving them a preferred home partway in from the surface.

**Segregation** is the chemical residue of the freeze: the last liquid — top center again — ends up enriched in carbon, sulfur, and phosphorus. Classic patterns include **A-segregates** and **V-segregates** in the columnar and central zones, and centerline segregation in continuously cast strands. Segregation is solid metal, not a void — remember that when we get to its ultrasonic response.

Finally, stress. The ingot cools unevenly, and the weak planes between columnar grains can tear, producing internal cracks called **hot tears** that tend to follow the columnar boundaries. Surface defects — scabs, laps, ripple, and surface cracks — are normally removed by scarfing or grinding before rolling, but anything missed gets rolled into seams downstream.

sections.caption: Ingot discontinuities: origin, typical home, and straight-beam signature.
rows: Primary / secondary pipe

Deoxidation products (alumina, silicates, sulfides)

Segregation

sections.heading: How Ingot Discontinuities Answer the Beam
sections.body:
As-cast structure is **coarse-grained**, and grains scatter sound. In the Rayleigh regime — grains much smaller than the wavelength — scattering rises roughly with the **fourth power of frequency**, so a modest frequency drop buys a large noise reduction. At 5 MHz a heavy bloom may be all grass and no back wall; at **1 to 2.25 MHz** the same metal becomes testable. The price is sensitivity: the longitudinal wavelength in steel at 1 MHz is about 5.9 mm, so apply the λ/2 rule of thumb before promising to find small reflectors. Where the surface allows, a larger-diameter probe recovers some beam concentration.

Response then follows orientation:

- **Pipe** lies along the ingot axis. A straight beam from the side strikes it broadside: a strong echo plus a shadowed, weakened back reflection.
- **Porosity** is many reflectors smaller than the beam: a low cluster of echoes, a falling back wall, no single dominant signal.
- **Inclusions** return sharp, isolated pips; a large exogenous slag mass can look like a genuine planar reflector.
- **Segregation** is acoustically quiet — solid metal of slightly different chemistry. Expect little or no echo; the clue may be only a local change in attenuation or background noise.
- **Hot tears** are planar: loud when the beam is normal to them, nearly invisible edge-on.

The **loss-of-back-reflection** technique — watching back-wall amplitude while scanning — catches scattering discontinuities that never produce a clean echo of their own.

sections.caption: Straight-beam screens from sound as-cast metal, centerline pipe, and a porosity cluster — note what happens to the back reflection in each case.

sections.title: Orientation rules everything
sections.body: Before scanning, sketch the product and ask which way each likely discontinuity lies. **Aim the beam normal to the expected plane** — a side scan strikes axial pipe broadside, while an end scan slides along it and sees almost nothing. One scan direction never tells the whole story in cast or wrought stock.

sections.heading: Evaluating Against Codes and Standards
sections.body:
Ingots are rarely tested fully as-cast except for large forging ingots; most examination happens at the **bloom, billet, slab, or first-forging stage**, after surfaces are conditioned and hot work has refined the grain enough to pass sound. The governing document is normally the **purchase specification** or a product standard it invokes, and the practice follows the familiar straight-beam pattern of documents such as ASTM A388 (heavy forgings) and E114 (straight-beam contact practice):

- **Reference level** set on flat-bottom-hole reference blocks (E127-style aluminum or E428-style steel sets) or as a percentage of the first back reflection in sound material.
- **Acceptance** keyed to indication amplitude versus the reference, to **complete or percentage loss of back reflection**, and to traveling or clustered indications.
- **Recording**: location, depth, amplitude relative to reference, estimated extent, and the back-reflection condition.

Two field cautions. First, confirm that grain noise still leaves the required signal-to-noise margin at the chosen frequency — a reference set on a fine-grained calibration block does not transfer honestly into coarse cast structure without a transfer-correction check. Second, document crop-end findings: pipe or gross segregation near an end often means the discard was short, and the customer's metallurgist will want to know.

checkQuestions.question: An ingot solidifies from the mold wall inward. Where do shrinkage pipe and heavy segregation concentrate, and why?
options: At the bottom corners, because cooling is slowest there
At the top center, because that region freezes last and collects the final shrinkage and rejected solute
Uniformly through the chill zone, where the metal froze first
Along the outer skin, where gas escapes most easily
checkQuestions.explanation: The last liquid to freeze sits at the top center: unfed shrinkage there becomes pipe, and the carbon, sulfur, and phosphorus rejected by the growing crystals concentrate in the same region.

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a fully killed ingot, a rimmed-steel ingot typically shows:
options: A deeper shrinkage pipe and almost no gas porosity
A clean low-carbon rim surrounding a core containing blowholes and heavy segregation
No segregation, because the rimming action stirs the melt uniform
Hydrogen flakes already present in the as-cast state
checkQuestions.explanation: Rimmed steel is barely deoxidized, so carbon monoxide evolves during freezing: the rimming action builds a clean rim while blowholes and solute concentrate in the core. Killed steel traps little gas but pays with a deeper pipe.

checkQuestions.question: A 5 MHz straight-beam examination of a heavy as-cast bloom shows grass across the whole baseline and no usable back wall. The standard first move is to:
options: Increase the gain until a back wall appears above the grass
Switch to a 10 MHz probe for better resolution
Drop to a lower frequency such as 1 to 2.25 MHz to reduce grain scattering
Switch to shear waves, which scatter less in coarse grain
checkQuestions.explanation: Grain scattering rises roughly with the fourth power of frequency, so lowering frequency restores signal-to-noise; adding gain amplifies the grass along with everything else, and shear waves have shorter wavelengths that scatter even more.

checkQuestions.question: Which ingot discontinuity commonly produces little or no direct echo, sometimes revealing itself only as a local change in attenuation or background noise?
options: Secondary pipe
An exogenous slag inclusion
Segregation
A hot tear oriented normal to the beam
checkQuestions.explanation: Segregation is solid metal with locally different chemistry rather than a void, so there is little impedance mismatch to reflect the beam — it is largely transparent to ultrasound.

checkQuestions.question: Scanning a bloom from the side, you see a cluster of small, low-amplitude echoes around mid-depth while the back reflection drops to about 20% with no single dominant flaw echo. The most likely cause is:
options: A single large planar crack normal to the beam
Porosity or a blowhole cluster scattering the beam
Roughness on the far surface
Mode conversion at the entry surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Many small reflectors smaller than the beam each return a weak echo while collectively robbing energy from the back wall — the classic porosity signature. A large planar crack would give one strong echo instead.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §2.1 (ingots: process review, discontinuities, response, codes/standards)
Steel solidification and primary-mill practice — general metallurgy references
Straight-beam practices and FBH reference sets per standards such as ASTM A388, E114, and E127/E428 (cited generically)
